links.reason: Smith's invisible hand explains how self-interest coordinates voluntary exchange — the antidote to the labor-theory-of-value.
Bastiat demolishes the illusion that redistribution creates wealth rather than merely moving it.
Division of labor and exchange explain rising productivity without surplus-value theft.
Housing mandates have seen beneficiaries and unseen shortages — read before defending rent caps.
Bastiat's test for legal plunder applies when the state seizes property rights in the name of affordability.
Mandatory wage floors have visible winners and unseen job losses at the margin.
Every mandated benefit is paid from somewhere — usually the worker who never gets hired.
Single-payer promises visible care while hiding queues, rationing, and tax burdens.
Bastiat shows how socialists use law to reorganize society — exactly what the USSR did.
Single-cause explanations ignore unseen institutional failures Bastiat teaches us to trace.
Smith's three duties of government clarify what Nordic states still leave to markets.
Voluntary exchange is not zero-sum — wealth creation is not theft from workers.
Read the actual enumerated limits and the Bill of Rights — not a paraphrase.
